sound_type: splash + song integrated -- three acoustic layers: stream broadband drone (below 2,000 Hz), bubbly song phrases (2,000-8,000 Hz), periodic splash transients (broadband). Pattern: sing-sing-SPLASH-silence (underwater)-SURFACE-sing. E=5.
ecosystem: Rapid creek -- fast-flowing mountain streams with rocky bottoms. Same habitat as degree 116 but focus shifts from the singing perch to the underwater foraging zone. Currents 0.5-2.0 m/s. Water temperature 0-4 degrees C.
season: Year-round resident. Dunking behavior occurs in all seasons. Foraging intensity varies with prey availability and breeding season demands.

coordinates.description: Reference for rapid creek habitat in the central Cascades. Same location as degree 116. Focus shifts from stream-side perch to underwater foraging zone.

taxonomy_note.description: SECOND AMERICAN DIPPER ENTRY -- degree 116 was the song (river singer), degree 117 is the dunking behavior (the plunge). Second species with two consecutive entries after Pileated Woodpecker (108-109). Both double-entries separate acoustic behavior from physical behavior.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Cinclidae count: 2 entries (American Dipper song 116, American Dipper dunking 117).
taxonomy_note.double_entry_note: Second species with consecutive double-entry. First: Pileated Woodpecker (108 laughing, 109 drumming). Second: American Dipper (116 song, 117 dunking). Both separate vocalization from physical behavior.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Cinclus -- same species, different behavioral focus. Degree 116 = acoustic signature. Degree 117 = biomechanical signature.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Cinclidae at degrees 116-117. The Cinclidae pair. Two degrees for one species -- the behavioral richness requires it.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Splash: broadband (20 Hz - 10,000+ Hz), concentrated below 1,000 Hz. Song: 2,000-8,000 Hz (same as degree 116). Stream noise: dominant below 2,000 Hz.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: Splash peak energy: 100-1,000 Hz (transient). Song peak energy: 3,000-6,000 Hz (intermittent). Stream peak energy: below 2,000 Hz (continuous).
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Three-layer integration: Stream drone (continuous, below 2,000 Hz) + Bubbly song (intermittent, 2,000-8,000 Hz) + Splash (periodic transient, broadband).
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Splash: 0.1-0.3 seconds. Underwater silence: 10-30 seconds. Song phrases: variable, intermittent. The cycle: sing (variable) -> splash (0.1-0.3s) -> underwater silence (10-30s) -> surface -> sing.
song_metrics.note_structure: SPLASH-SONG INTEGRATION: Song phrases punctuated by splash transients. The splash is the acoustic boundary between singer and diver. Three layers: rhythm (periodic splashes), melody (bubbly song), harmony (stream drone).
song_metrics.timbre: Splash: sharp, bright, transient -- water impact. Song: bubbly, musical, complex (same as degree 116). Combined: percussive accents within a flowing musical stream.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5 -- reflects the physical energy of submersion. The plunge into fast-flowing mountain water is an athletic act. Overcoming buoyancy, resisting current, gripping rocks, foraging, returning to surface. Enormous energy expenditure for a 60-gram bird.
song_metrics.context: FORAGING + TERRITORIAL. The splash-song integration occurs during active foraging with territorial singing. The bird alternates between two behavioral states: singing (territory) and foraging (food). The splash marks the transition.
song_metrics.key_property: SPLASH + SONG INTEGRATED -- three acoustic layers unique in PNW soundscape. SECOND CONSECUTIVE DIPPER ENTRY. Degree 116 = song. Degree 117 = dunking. The plunge: walking underwater against fast current using wing hydrofoils. 10-30 second dives. 0-4C water. Caddisfly/mayfly/stonefly larvae + salmon eggs. E=5 = energy of submersion.

plumage.overall: Same as degree 116. Stocky, uniformly dark gray. Short tail. But degree 117 focuses on the bird underwater -- the plumage's waterproofing function is paramount here.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 117: The bird underwater. Dense, oiled plumage sheds water instantly on surfacing. Air trapped in plumage creates silvery bubble appearance when submerged. The bird glows faintly underwater as light refracts through the air layer.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Minimal. Year-round waterproofing maintained by constant preening with oversized preen gland.
plumage.flight_pattern: Low, fast, direct along stream corridors. At degree 117: the transition from flight to plunge -- the moment the bird hits the water surface.

nodes.title: The Plunge -- Underwater Foraging Behavior
nodes.description: The dipper's signature dunking: walk-in, direct dive, or surface swim entry into fast-flowing water. Walks along stream bottom against current. Forages for 10-30 seconds. Surfaces dry. Three entry modes, one purpose: access benthic prey in fast current.

nodes.title: Wing Hydrofoils -- Hydrodynamic Solution
nodes.description: Partially opened wings angled against current generate downforce (negative lift). Inverted airplane wings underwater. Faster current = more downforce = more grip. Combined with claw adhesion, streamlined posture, and buoyancy management. The bird uses the river's own energy to stay on the bottom.

nodes.title: Splash-Song Integration -- Three Acoustic Layers
nodes.description: Three layers: stream drone (continuous, below 2,000 Hz) + bubbly song (intermittent, 2,000-8,000 Hz) + splash transients (periodic, broadband). Pattern: sing-SPLASH-silence-SURFACE-sing. Rhythm (splashes), melody (song), harmony (stream). Unique in PNW soundscape.

nodes.title: Double-Entry Pattern -- Cinclidae Pair (116-117)
nodes.description: Second species with two consecutive entries. First: Pileated WP (108 laughing, 109 drumming). Second: American Dipper (116 song, 117 dunking). Both separate vocal from physical behavior. Behaviorally rich species need two degrees.

nodes.title: Benthic Foraging -- Salmon Connection
nodes.description: Primary prey: caddisfly, mayfly, stonefly larvae between rocks. Seasonal: salmon eggs from redds. The dipper's foraging connects it to the salmon cycle central to PNW ecology. Water temperature 0-4C. Dives to 1 meter depth.

cross_references.relationship: CONSECUTIVE PAIR. Song (116) and dunking (117). Acoustic vs. physical. Singing over water (116) vs. going into water (117). Second double-entry after Pileated WP (108-109).
DOUBLE-ENTRY PRECEDENT. First species with two entries: laughing (108) + drumming (109). Dipper mirrors: song (116) + dunking (117). Vocalization separated from physical act.
PHYSICAL BEHAVIOR PARALLEL. Degree 109: bill strikes wood. Degree 117: body enters water. Both physical behaviors producing distinctive sounds. Drumming: percussive resonance. Dunking: splash.
WATER CONTINUATION. Fire-to-water began at 115-116. Degree 117 goes deeper -- literally. The aquatic commitment deepens from singing over water to walking through it.
MOUNTAIN STREAM CONNECTION. Both montane. Chickadee in canopy above; dipper underwater below. Degree 117 emphasizes the aquatic niche the chickadee merely flies over.
